title: Panna cotta
subtitle: Italian set cream with vanilla
excerpt: Cream, sugar, and vanilla set with just enough gelatine to hold their shape when unmoulded, then turned out onto a plate to reveal a soft, barely-trembling cream that collapses at the spoon. One of the simplest elegant desserts. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Panna cotta (cooked cream) is a Piedmontese dessert documented in Italian cookery from the early 20th century. The dessert is set with gelatine rather than eggs, which gives it a lighter, more refined texture than a custard. Documented by Marcella Hazan in The Classic Italian Cook Book (1973) and by Anna Del Conte in Gastronomy of Italy (1987). The use of leaf gelatine rather than powdered gives a cleaner, less rubbery result.

content.text: Panna cotta is one of the few desserts where the goal is the minimum amount of setting agent that still works. Too much gelatine and the result is rubbery, like a commercial dessert. The right amount produces a cream that barely holds its shape. It should tremble and threaten to collapse when unmoulded, and melt into a puddle at room temperature within minutes. The flavour should be cream, vanilla, and very little else.

items.name: Gelatine leaves
items.prepNote: soaked in cold water for 5 minutes

content.text: Soak {{gelatine-leaves}} gelatine leaves in cold water for 5 minutes until floppy. Meanwhile, pour {{double-cream}} of double cream and {{whipping-cream}} of whipping cream into a saucepan. Add {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and the vanilla pod and its scraped seeds. Heat over medium heat, stirring, until the sugar dissolves and the cream just begins to steam. Do not boil. Remove from the heat. Squeeze the soaked gelatine firmly to remove excess water, then add to the hot cream. Stir until fully dissolved. Remove the vanilla pod. Pour through a fine sieve into a jug. Lightly oil four 150 ml ramekins or dariole moulds with a neutral oil. Pour in the cream mixture. Cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours until fully set. To unmould, run a knife around the edge of each panna cotta and invert onto a plate. Serve with seasonal fruit or a little honey.

items.fix: Three standard leaves is correct for this volume. Each leaf is 2 g, weigh them if the packet sizing is unfamiliar. More than 3 leaves makes a set solid enough to bounce.
items.cause: Too much gelatine, or gelatine not measured by weight.
items.symptom: Panna cotta set too firmly and rubbery

items.fix: Oil the mould lightly with a neutral oil. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours. To release, run a thin palette knife around the edge and give the mould a firm sideways shake before inverting.
items.cause: Mould not oiled, or panna cotta not fully set.
items.symptom: Won't unmould cleanly, tears or collapses

items.fix: The cream must be hot when the squeezed gelatine goes in. Stir for a full 2 minutes. Pass through a sieve to catch any undissolved pieces.
items.cause: Gelatine added to cream that was too cool, so it didn't dissolve fully.
items.symptom: Cream and gelatine separated in the mould

content.text: Panna cotta is restrained on its own and asks for a sauce or fruit alongside. Seasonal berries macerated in a little sugar are the classic accompaniment. A blood orange sauce in winter, a strawberry compote in summer, a drizzle of bitter honey at any time of year. The cream is very mild and takes well to acidic or bitter contrasts.
